---
title: "Workshop R spatial"
author: "Team inSileco"
date: "2026-03-25"
from: markdown+emoji
---

```{=html}
<script>
document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function() {
  var logo = document.querySelector('.slide-logo');
  if (logo) {
    var link = document.createElement('a');
    link.href = 'https://www.insileco.io';
    link.target = '_blank';
    logo.parentNode.insertBefore(link, logo);
    link.appendChild(logo);
  }
});
</script>
```

# Day 1


# Welcome 

<!-- Go around the table + slide insileco? -->
<!-- Why do you expect  -->


# R as a GIS: Why? 

<!-- Discuss why it makes sense to use R -->



## R to build reproducible pipelines

- integrate many tools: 
  - C library: e.g. 
  - read many file 
  - do analysis
  - write report 


<!-- Ideally a nice diagram with a workflow -->

::: {.notes}
Could be python etc...
:::


## A note about IDEs

<!-- show them? -->

- RStudio 
- VSCode 
- Positron 


## A note about geocomputation 

- General introduction 
  - https://r.geocompx.org/
  - https://sci1031.github.io/sci1031/


::: {.callout-caution}
We won't cover general 'what's the best projection for that' we will cover 'how I can change the projection withR'
:::




## Main packages

- Before `sp` + `raster`
- Now
  - `sf` + `stars`
  - `terra` (+ tidyterra)

## Main packages 

- A nice level of abstraction to work with spatial data 

- `sf` GEOS and GDAL PROJ, S2Geometry for spherical operations (on longitude/latitude data).

- `terra` does planar geometry using GEOS and GDAL.

For raster operations, terra relies on PROJ and GDAL rather than S2.


:::footer
[r-spatial](https://github.com/r-spatial)
&nbsp; ***·*** &nbsp;
[Geocomputation with R](https://r.geocompx.org/)
:::


# `sf`

## Simple features 

> Simple features or simple feature access refers to a formal standard (ISO 19125-1:2004) that describes how objects in the real world can be represented in computers, with emphasis on the spatial geometry of these objects. It also describes how such objects can be stored in and retrieved from databases, and which geometrical operations should be defined for them.

> The standard is widely implemented in spatial databases (such as PostGIS), commercial GIS (e.g., ESRI ArcGIS) and forms the vector data basis for libraries such as GDAL. A subset of simple features forms the GeoJSON standard.

:::footer
[Simple features for R](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/sf/vignettes/sf1.html)
:::


## Simple features from scratch

<!-- do we want to go theren?? -->

- One point

```{R}
#| echo: true
library(sf)
st_point(c(-71.2080, 46.8139))
```


## Simple features from scratch

- 3 points within a collection 


```{R}
geom <- st_sfc(
  st_point(c(-71.2080, 46.8139)),  # Québec
  st_point(c(-71.2500, 46.8200)),
  st_point(c(-71.1800, 46.8000)),
  crs = 4326
)
geom
```


## Simple features from scratch

A data frame with attributes

```{R}
df <- data.frame(
  bird = c("Snow Goose", "Common Eider", "Herring Gull"),
  date = as.Date(c("2025-04-12", "2025-04-13", "2025-04-14"))
)

# 3. Combine attributes and geometry into an sf object
birds_sf <- st_sf(df, geometry = geom)

birds_sf
```


## Reading and writting vector files 

- `st_read()`
- `st_write()`

<!-- Add R example  -->


## Projection 

- `st_crs()`
- `st_transform()`

<!-- Add R example  -->



## Exercise 1: R as a converter 

Create a function that 
- read different files 
- write in geojson in 4326

- advanced? conversion using crs and format of a second file



## Visualisation 

- `plot()`
- `mapview()`


## Buffer

- `st_buffer()`

<!-- Add R example  -->


## Distance 

- `st_distance()`
- `st_length()`

<!-- Add R example  -->


## Overalap and co 

- `st_union()`
- `st_within()`
- `st_crosses()`


## simplify 

<!-- Add R example  -->


## tidyverse flow 

<!-- Add R example  -->

- group by / union
- data frame 


## Exercise 2 

- read this 
- do that manip 
- save as this 


# `stars`


## `stars` 

Spatiotemporal Arrays: Raster and Vector Datacubes


## Example 

```{R}
library(dplyr)
library(stars)
tif = system.file("tif/L7_ETMs.tif", package = "stars")
read_stars(tif) |>
  slice(index = 1, along = "band") |>
  plot()
```


## read and write 

<!-- Add R example  -->

## rewrap 

<!-- Add R example  -->

## extract 

<!-- Add R example  -->

## crop 



# `terra`


## `terra`

Redo everything with terra 



# Exercise 4 






# Day 2

## Note 

https://sci1031.github.io/sci1031/lecon_intro.html


- https://geojson.io/next/
- https://r-spatial.org/r/2023/09/21/stars-parallel.html
- https://gallery.shinyapps.io/assistant
- Claude agent 
- https://github.com/geoarrow/geoarrow-r
- https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/rpostgis/index.html
- https://sedona.apache.org/latest/api/rdocs/articles/apache-sedona.html

- Create an app that allows users to upload GeoJSON files and visualize them using Leaflet.
- Modify the app to compute the total area when polygons are uploaded and display it in the card header.
- Modify the app to also compute the total length for line geometries and display it in the card header.
